text: But the {{c1::economic impact seems}}(经济影响似乎) to be {{c2::dramatically behind}}(远远落后). It’s very difficult to {{c3::make sense of}}(理解), how can the model, {{c4::on the one hand}}(一方面), do these amazing things, and then {{c5::on the other hand}}(另一方面), repeat itself twice in some situation?
original: But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to make sense of, how can the model, on the one hand, do these amazing things, and then on the other hand, repeat itself twice in some situation?
chunk_text: Ilya Sutskever Yeah. This is one of the very confusing things about the models right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.” They are doing so well. But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to make sense of, how can the model, on the one hand, do these amazing things, and then on the other hand, repeat itself twice in some situation?
expression_transfer: Useful for organizing argument flow across longer explanations.

text: But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to {{c1::make sense of}}(理解), how can the model, {{c2::on the one hand}}(一方面), do these amazing things, and then {{c3::on the other hand}}(另一方面), {{c4::repeat itself twice}}(重复两次) in some situation?

text: There’ll be very strong {{c1::economic forces for}}(经济力量) this, and I think the {{c2::impact is going}}(影响正在) to be felt very strongly. Dwarkesh Patel When do you {{c3::expect that impact}}(预计会产生影响)?
original: There’ll be very strong economic forces for this, and I think the impact is going to be felt very strongly. Dwarkesh Patel When do you expect that impact?
chunk_text: Ilya Sutskever The thing which I was referring to not feeling different is, okay, such and such company announced some difficult-to-comprehend dollar amount of investment. I don’t think anyone knows what to do with that. But I think the impact of AI is going to be felt. AI is going to be diffused through the economy. There’ll be very strong economic forces for this, and I think the impact is going to be felt very strongly. Dwarkesh Patel When do you expect that impact? I think the models seem smarter than their economic impact would imply. Ilya Sutskever
expression_transfer: Useful for signaling your stance before giving evidence.

text: The idea that {{c1::we’d be investing}}(我们会投入) 1% of GDP in AI, {{c2::I feel like}}(我觉得) it would have felt like {{c3::a bigger deal}}(更大的事), whereas {{c4::right now}}(现在) it just feels... Ilya Sutskever
original: The idea that we’d be investing 1% of GDP in AI, I feel like it would have felt like a bigger deal, whereas right now it just feels... Ilya Sutskever
chunk_text: Ilya Sutskever — We're moving from the age of scaling to the age of research – Explaining model jaggedness Ilya Sutskever You know what’s crazy? That all of this is real. Dwarkesh Patel Meaning what? Ilya Sutskever Don’t you think so? All this AI stuff and all this Bay Area… that it’s happening. Isn’t it straight out of science fiction? Dwarkesh Patel Another thing that’s crazy is how normal the slow takeoff feels. The idea that we’d be investing 1% of GDP in AI, I feel like it would have felt like a bigger deal, whereas right now it just feels... Ilya Sutskever
expression_transfer: Useful for acknowledging a point before pivoting to contrast.

text: Then you {{c1::tell the model}}(告诉模型), “Can you {{c2::please fix the bug}}(请修复这个错误)?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right. I have a bug. Let me {{c3::go fix that}}(去修复那个).”
original: Then you tell the model, “Can you please fix the bug?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right. I have a bug. Let me go fix that.”
chunk_text: An example would be, let’s say you use vibe coding to do something. You go to some place and then you get a bug. Then you tell the model, “Can you please fix the bug?” And the model says, “Oh my God, you’re so right. I have a bug. Let me go fix that.” And it introduces a second bug. Then you tell it, “You have this new second bug,” and it tells you, “Oh my God, how could I have done it? You’re so right again,” and brings back the first bug, and you can alternate between those. How is that possible? I’m not sure, but it does suggest that something strange is going on.
expression_transfer: Useful as a reusable chunk for fluent topic transitions.

text: This is one of the {{c1::very confusing things}}(非常令人困惑的事情) {{c2::about the models}}(关于这些模型) right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are {{c3::doing so well}}(表现这么好) on evals?
original: This is one of the very confusing things about the models right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals?
chunk_text: Ilya Sutskever Yeah. This is one of the very confusing things about the models right now. How to reconcile the fact that they are doing so well on evals? You look at the evals and you go, “Those are pretty hard evals.” They are doing so well. But the economic impact seems to be dramatically behind. It’s very difficult to make sense of, how can the model, on the one hand, do these amazing things, and then on the other hand, repeat itself twice in some situation?
expression_transfer: Useful as a reusable chunk for fluent topic transitions.

text: Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of {{c1::what data to train}}(要训练什么数据) on was answered, because that answer was everything. When you do pre-training, you need all the data.
original: Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of what data to train on was answered, because that answer was everything. When you do pre-training, you need all the data.
chunk_text: I have two possible explanations. The more whimsical explanation is that maybe RL training makes the models a little too single-minded and narrowly focused, a little bit too unaware, even though it also makes them aware in some other ways. Because of this, they can’t do basic things. But there is another explanation. Back when people were doing pre-training, the question of what data to train on was answered, because that answer was everything. When you do pre-training, you need all the data. So you don’t have to think if it’s going to be this data or that data.
expression_transfer: Useful as a reusable chunk for fluent topic transitions.
